titlestmt.titleproper: Preliminary Guide to the Joseph Goldstein Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Mike Strom and Robert Bartels

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.
This collection contains 1 published digital object.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday March 31, 2022 at 07:02

did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence, topical files, research files, writings, speeches, and course materials that document the career of Joseph Goldstein.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Sonja Goldstein, 2000 and 2019. Accession 2021-M-0020 transferred from Yale Law School, 2021.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright is retained by the Donor of this collection for materials authored or otherwise produced by Joseph Goldstein. After the lifetime of the Donor, copyright passes to Yale University. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The majority of the materials are open for research. Student records and personnel records are restricted until 2097 as established by Yale Corporation regulations. http://web.library.yale.edu/mssa/collections/research-use-of-yale-university-archives.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Joseph Goldstein Papers (MS 1787). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Joseph Goldstein was born in 1923 in Springfield, Massachusetts. He received a bachelor's degree from Dartmouth in 1943 and entered military service during WWII. He served in the Civil Censorship Detachment of the Occupation General Headquarters in Occupied Japan where, as part of the Theatre Sub-Section, he evaluated and censored Japanese theatre productions and encouraged new shows featuring democratic themes. Following his military service, he completed his doctorate at the London School of Economics. There, he worked closely with Harold Laski writing a dissertation on British trade unions, earning his degree in 1950. He and his spouse, Sonja Goldstein, then entered the Yale School of Law. While in law school, the Goldsteins collaborated on a project studying the impact of the anti-communist publication Red Channels on those it named as communists.

Joseph Goldstein specialized in family law and is credited with being among the first to introduce interdisciplinary thinking to the legal profession. He taught at Yale Law School from 1956 to 2000 and authored or co-authored books in the areas of criminal law, constitutional law, and the intersection of law, psychiatry, and psychoanalysis. Joseph Goldstein died on March 12, 2000, in New Haven, Connecticut.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers consist of correspondence, topical files, research files, writings, speeches, and course materials that document the career of Joseph Goldstein. The papers include notes and correspondence from Anna Freud, with whom Goldstein co-authored three books, and records from projects he was involved with outside the legal profession, such as Friends of the Library of the Supreme Court of Israel. The papers include material on Goldstein's work in allied-occupied Japan in the 1940s as part of the Civil Censorship Detachment, Theatre Sub-Section; research files related to his work at the Yale Law School and his study of the anti-communist publication Red Channels ; and correspondence documenting Goldstein's collaboration with economist Harold Laski. Additionally, the papers contain information on Goldstein's work with local New Haven legal organizations such as the New Haven Legal Assistance Association (NHLAA), as well as files related to the Yale Law School.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Original accession and two additions. Accession 2020-M-0016 is arranged in four groupings: Allied occupation of Japan: Civil Censorship Detachment (CCD), Theatre Sub-Section; Yale Law School; Harold Laski and London School of Economics; and Legal career. Accession 2020-M-0021 is arranged in three groupings: New Haven organizations, Personal files, and Yale Law School.

did.unittitle: Anna Freud - Family Law II Seminar, 1968

altformavail.head: Existence and Location of Copies
altformavail.p: A copy of this material is available in digital form from Manuscripts and Archives. Contact Manuscripts and Archives at mssa.assist@yale.edu to request access to the digital copy.

did.unittitle: Course materials [lecture notes, instructions for assignments]

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The accession is arranged in four groupings: Allied Occupation of Japan: Civil Censorship Detachment (CCD), Theatre Sub-Section; Yale Law School; Harold Laski and London School of Economics; and Legal career.

did.unittitle: Allied Occupation of Japan: Civil Censorship Detachment (CCD), Theatre Sub-Section

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Materials in this grouping document Goldstein's service as part of the Theatre Sub-Section of the Civil Censorship Detachment, part of the Occupation General Headquarters (GHQ) in Tokyo, 1945 to 1946.

did.unittitle: Campaign to save the Office of Price Administration
Civil Information and Education Section, Media Analysis Division, analysis reports
Civil Information and Education Section, Media Analysis Division, analysis reports
Japanese language reference materials, guides, maps, and publications
Grammar of Modern Written Japanese , 3rd edition

title.part: War and Construction: A Pictorial Record of the Second Year of the War of Greater East Asia

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Materials in this grouping document Joseph and Sonja Goldstein's time as students in the Yale Law School. The grouping consists primarily of course materials and extensive files related to their research project "Zeitgeist: An Exploratory Study of the Causes and Impact of Red Channels with Special Emphasis on Bigotry—A Problem in Psychological and Social Causation." For the study, the Goldsteins surveyed individuals in arts and entertainment who had been named as communists by the publication Red Channels about the impact it had on their lives and careers. Respondents to the survey included: Oscar Brand, Himan Brown, Vera Caspary, Ed Chodorov, Norman Corwin, Olin Downes, Shirley Graham Du Bois, Clifford Durr, Will Geer, Uta Hagen, Langston Hughes, Leon Janney, George Keane, Millard Lampell, Madeleine Lee, Samson Raphaelson, Anne Revere, Norman Rosten, William Shirer, Howard K. Smith, Robert St. John, Lionel Stander, Louis Untermeyer, Hilda Vaughn, and Ireene Wicker.

did.unittitle: Business units I and future interests
Procedure IA, torts, introduction to public law, contracts
Harold Laski and London School of Economics

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This grouping includes correspondence documenting Joseph Goldstein's relationship with Harold Laski, with whom he worked as a student at the London School of Economics, as well as other materials related to Laski that were collected by Goldstein. Also included are materials related to Goldstein's research on British trade unions, personal correspondence between Joseph and Sonja Goldstein and Harold and Frida Laski, letters of introduction written by Laski for Joseph Goldstein, and correspondence and a transcript related to a 1961 BBC radio program about Laski, for which Goldstein was interviewed. Other Laski-related materials include: publications and manuscripts, newspaper clippings, and correspondence between Laski and other individuals. Included in the correspondence is a brief 1922 note to Laski from the poet Siegfried Sassoon.

did.unittitle: Goldstein, Joseph and Sonja, with Harold and Frida Laski
Goldstein, Joseph, with Felix Frankfurter regarding Harold Laski
Laski, Harold, and Louis D. Brandeis
Unidentified correspondent to Felix Frankfurter, regarding the Guffey Act, Carter v. Carter Coal Company, and John L. Lewis

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The unidentified author's first name is likely John, possibly someone who knew or studied with Frankfurter while he was a visiting professor at Oxford (1933-1934).

did.unittitle: Data on British trade union votes
Reports on antisemitism in Britain, Nazi occupation of Poland and Holland, and life in Terezín, Czechoslovakia
"The Theory of the Rule of Law and England"
Goldstein, Joseph, "Best interests of the child" short writings and fragments
Goldstein, Joseph, "The collective bargaining movement in the U.S." outline and readings
Goldstein, Joseph, correspondence with Victor Reuther
Goldstein, Joseph, notes on interest in law
Goldstein seminar, Research and Training Institute for Family Court Probation Officers, and related materials on juveniles and criminal justice (United States and Japan)
Smith v. Organization of Foster Families for Equality and Reform (431 US816)

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The majority of the materials are open for research. Student records and personnel files are restricted until 2097 as established by Yale Corporation regulations. http://web.library.yale.edu/mssa/collections/research-use-of-yale-university-archives.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The accession contains material documenting Joseph Goldstein's work with local New Haven organizations, particularly the New Haven Legal Assistance Association (NHLAA), which provides legal services for New Haven County residents unable to afford or maintain legal counsel. Goldstein helped fund the organization in 1964 and served on its board from 1974 until his death in 2000. The accession also contains material relating to the Yale Law School, including various boards and committees Goldstein served on, as well as Goldstein's personal files.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The accession is arranged in two groupings: New Haven organizations and Yale Law School.

did.unittitle: Connecticut Consortium of Legal Aid Contributions
Committee on institutional foundation and individual gifts
New York Knicks basketball exhibition game
South Central Connecticut Legal Services Corporation

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains correspondence and memorandums regarding political and legal issues and their potential effects on Yale Law School faculty.
